content:
# Matthew Chapter 28 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 28 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 28:1 — "toward the dawn of the first day of the week" vs. "at dawn on Sunday morning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "after [the] Sabbaths, at the dawning toward the first [day] of [the] week" | Ultra-literal, preserving the plural "Sabbaths" and the Jewish reckoning |
| LOGOS | "after the Sabbath, toward the dawn of the first day of the week" | Formal, preserving the Jewish temporal framework |
| PNEUMA | "After the Sabbath, at dawn on Sunday morning" | Dynamic — replaces "first day of the week" with "Sunday" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. The phrase "first day of the week" (μίαν σαββάτων) is the earliest Christian designation for the day of resurrection. Replacing it with "Sunday" (PNEUMA) imports a later naming convention and loses the Jewish temporal framework within which the resurrection occurs. The resurrection happened within the Jewish week — "the first day" after the Sabbath.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The Jewish temporal framework is preserved.

---

## 28:2 — "there was a great earthquake" vs. "there was a violent earthquake" vs. "Suddenly there was a violent earthquake"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a great earthquake happened" | Literal — σεισμὸς μέγας |
| LOGOS | "there was a great earthquake" | Formal English |
| PNEUMA | "Suddenly there was a violent earthquake" | Dynamic — adds "suddenly" and renders μέγας as "violent" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "there was a great earthquake" (LOGOS). "Great" is the natural rendering of μέγας and preserves the connection to the earthquake at the crucifixion (27:54). PNEUMA's "suddenly" is an addition (though ἰδού in the preceding clause has a similar function), and "violent" changes the connotation from magnitude to destructiveness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 28:6 — "he has been raised" (passive voice) — ἠγέρθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he was raised just as he said" | Passive voice preserved |
| LOGOS | "he has been raised, as he said" | Passive voice preserved, perfect tense |
| PNEUMA | "He has been raised from the dead, just as he said would happen" | Passive voice preserved, with addition "from the dead" and "would happen" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three correctly preserve the passive voice ἠγέρθη — God is the agent of resurrection. SOPHIA adopted LOGOS phrasing. PNEUMA adds "from the dead" (which is in v.7 but not v.6 in Sinaiticus) and "would happen" (not in the Greek). These additions clarify but expand beyond the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Passive voice unanimously preserved — this is non-negotiable.

---

## 28:9 — "Greetings!" for χαίρετε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Greetings!" | Standard rendering, with note that literal meaning is "Rejoice!" |
| LOGOS | "Greetings!" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Greetings!" | Same, with note on the ironic parallel to Judas's χαῖρε in 26:49 |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three render χαίρετε as "Greetings!" — the everyday quality of the risen Jesus' first word is part of its power. EMET's note on the literal "Rejoice!" and PNEUMA's observation about the Judas parallel (26:49) are both valuable. The same word used by the betrayer in the garden is now spoken by the risen Lord at the tomb — the greeting that sealed Jesus' arrest now inaugurates his resurrection.

**Status:** Full agreement. Notes from all three translators incorporated.

---

## 28:11 — "some of the guard" vs. "some of the guards"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "some of the guard" | Collective singular — τῆς κουστωδίας |
| LOGOS | "some of the guard" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "some of the guards" | Plural — interpreting the collective as individuals |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "some of the guard" (LOGOS). The Greek κουστωδία is a collective term (a guard unit), not a plural of individual guards. "The guard" preserves the military unit terminology.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor grammatical point.

---

## 28:15 — "among the Jews" vs. "among the Jewish people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "among [the] Ioudaioi" | Transliterated Greek |
| LOGOS | "among the Jews" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "among the Jewish people" | Expanded for sensitivity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "among the Jews" (LOGOS). The Greek Ἰουδαίοις means "Jews/Judeans." LOGOS's rendering is direct and accurate. PNEUMA's "Jewish people" is a modern expansion not in the Greek. SOPHIA notes that this verse describes a specific historical polemic (an anti-Christian counter-narrative in the first century) and should be read in that context, not as a generalized statement about Jewish people.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 28:17 — "but some doubted" — preservation of this remarkable admission

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "but some doubted" | Literal — οἱ δὲ ἐδίστασαν |
| LOGOS | "but some doubted" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "though some of them still had doubts" | Dynamic — softens with "still had doubts" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "but some doubted" (LOGOS). The Greek is stark: οἱ δὲ ἐδίστασαν — "but they doubted." PNEUMA's "still had doubts" is softer and suggests lingering rather than active doubt. The Greek verb δίσταζω means "to be of two minds" (the same word used of Peter wavering on the water in 14:31). The brevity and starkness should be preserved — this is one of Matthew's most honest sentences.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The admission's force depends on its brevity.

---

## 28:18 — "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All authority was given to me in heaven and upon earth" | Literal word order following the Greek |
| LOGOS | "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" | Formal English with inverted word order |
| PNEUMA | "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA word order. Placing "in heaven and on earth" after "all authority" creates the sweeping scope before naming the recipient. This is one of the most important declarations in the NT and the word order affects its impact.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Word order adjusted for English impact.

---

## 28:19 — "make disciples of all nations" — grammatical structure

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Going therefore, make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them..." | Preserves the main imperative (μαθητεύσατε) with attendant participles |
| LOGOS | "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them..." | Formal English; renders πορευθέντες as a coordinate imperative |
| PNEUMA | "Therefore, go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them..." | Dynamic; same structure as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. EMET correctly identifies that the main imperative is μαθητεύσατε ("make disciples") while "going," "baptizing," and "teaching" are participles that describe how discipleship is accomplished. However, rendering πορευθέντες as "Go" (imperative in English) is appropriate for natural English and follows established convention. The theological point — that disciple-making is the central command — is noted in council records.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's grammatical insight is preserved in notes.

---

## 28:20 — "to the end of the age" vs. "to the very end of the age" vs. "all the days until the completion of the age"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all the days until the completion of the age" | Ultra-literal — πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας ἕως τῆς συντελείας τοῦ αἰῶνος |
| LOGOS | "always, to the end of the age" | Formal — "always" for "all the days" |
| PNEUMA | "always, to the very end of the age" | Dynamic — adds "very" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "always, to the end of the age" (LOGOS). EMET's "all the days" is the literal rendering and beautifully captures the dailiness of Jesus' promised presence — not just "always" in the abstract but "all the days," each one. However, "always" is the more natural English rendering and conveys the same scope. PNEUMA's "very" is an addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "all the days" is noted as a particularly beautiful literal rendering.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA additions beyond the Greek | 3 | Additions not adopted |
| Jewish temporal/cultural framework preservation | 2 | Jewish framework retained (first day of the week, the guard) |
| Grammatical precision (passive voice, imperative) | 2 | Greek grammar honored |
| Word order for English impact | 1 | English rhetorical order preferred |
| Vocabulary softening | 1 | Starkness preserved (doubted) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 28 — the resurrection and Great Commission — is the theological climax of the Gospel. The translators show the strongest consensus here, reflecting the gravity of the text. The most significant issues are: (1) the unanimous preservation of the passive "has been raised" (v.6), which maintains God as the agent of resurrection; (2) the preservation of "but some doubted" (v.17) in its stark brevity; and (3) the structure of the Great Commission itself (vv.18-20). SOPHIA notes that this final chapter forms an inclusio with 1:23 ("Emmanuel — God with us"): the Gospel that begins with "God with us" ends with "I am with you always." All three translators recognize this, and the consensus translation honors it.

---

*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, Bible Translation Project*
